steps.title: What this function answers
steps.narrative:
Given a candidate replacement pGraph (right) and a current root pRoot (left, with MFFC labeled), return how many AIG ANDs would actually be added if we materialized the graph — taking strash-table reuse into account.

This is the number every commit-or-skip decision sits on top of: gain = MFFC size − count.

panes.title: Setup
panes.body: (1) Cut leaves' pFunc fields are pre-set to the AIG nodes that play those roles in the materialized graph. (2) The MFFC of pRoot is labeled with the current TravId (§MFFC).

steps.title: Setup: rewrite chose cut {a, c, d, n6}
steps.narrative: From the §rewrite trace: the chosen cut at f is {a, c, d, n6}. The MFFC of f w.r.t. that cut is {f, n8, n7, n9} — those four ANDs are TravId-tagged. The candidate Dec_Graph (right) has 3 internal nodes m1, m2, m3 over the same 4 leaves.

entries.m3: OR(m1, m2, n6) — implements n6 + ad + cd

steps.title: Visit m1 = a · d → strash hits n7, but n7 ∈ MFFC → ADD
steps.narrative: Abc_AigAndLookup(a, d) hits n7. But Abc_NodeIsTravIdCurrent(n7) returns true — n7 is in the MFFC and will be deleted post-commit. The strash-table hit is illusory. Treat as add. count = 1.

steps.title: Visit m2 = c · d → hits n9, also in MFFC → ADD
steps.narrative: Same as m1 but for n9. count = 2.

steps.title: Visit m3 = OR(m1, m2, n6) — no strash match → ADD
steps.narrative: m3's structure (combining m1, m2, n6) doesn't match any existing AND. Abc_AigAndLookup misses → add. count = 3. Note: m3 is built as a small AND-tree internally — the "Cone = 3" in the rewrite trace counts those binary ANDs.

steps.title: Return: nNodesAdded = 3 → gain = 4 − 3 = 1
steps.narrative: Final answer: 3 ANDs would be added. Combined with MFFC = 4, gain = 1 → accept (under default fUseZeros=0).

panes.title: When this function returns -1
panes.body: Two cases: (a) the running count exceeds NodeMax = MFFC size — bail out early; (b) the new top-level node would equal pRoot structurally (a self-loop, decAbc.c:196..197) — also bail. Both are short-circuits; not all candidates need full evaluation.

steps.title: Why MFFC tagging matters
steps.narrative: Without the TravId check, rewrite would happily "reuse" n7 and n9 from the existing strash table, claim count = 1, gain = 3, and commit. But after committing, n7 and n9 — being inside the original MFFC — get deallocated by reference counting, leaving dangling pointers in the new graph. The TravId check at decAbc.c:202 is the safety belt: it forces the count to reflect what will actually exist post-commit.

panes.body: Same logic governs refactor (abcRefactor.c:207) and the various candidate-scoring sites in resub. All three operators commit only if Dec_GraphToNetworkCount's return strictly beats their nMinSaved threshold.
